Buy-In Business Portfolio · Case Files

Franchise Case Files

All 656 buy-in businesses, each as its own deep-dive file. Tap any to open the full breakdown - fee, total cost, royalty, track record, owner profit, staffing, customer acquisition, and territory - with your gate, survival, model-type, and hands-off tags up top. Figures are screening estimates; the definitive numbers live in each brand's FDD.

CASE FILE 001▶

Amazon DSP

Routes & distributorships · Vol I TOP FIT
Buy-in fee
no franchise fee
All-in cost
~$10K start
Royalty+ad
n/a - contract-based
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
$30K
CONTRACT GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: liquid $30K
01WHAT IT IS
Amazon last-mile delivery contractor program (not a franchise); operators run branded van fleets delivering packages, with 3,000+ DSPs nationwide.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: no franchise fee. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around ~$10K start. Startup ~$10,000; ~$30,000 liquid required; vans leased through Amazon at discounted rates; no route purchase.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Launched 2018; 4,400+ owners, 390,000 driving jobs, $58B total partner revenue per Amazon. But 1-year contracts, total single-customer dependence, and Oct 2025 reporting on DSPs quitting over rising costs and meager profit. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Amazon advertises $1M-$4.5M revenue and $75K-$300K profit potential for a 20-40 van fleet. Reality is thin: one detailed operator case showed $3.2M revenue on 25 routes yielding ~$80K take-home (~2.5% net); payroll runs 55-65% of revenue.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - contract-based. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 002▶

FedEx Ground routes (ISP)

Routes & distributorships · Vol I TOP FIT
Buy-in fee
$0
All-in cost
$250K-$1.5M+
Royalty+ad
n/a - contract-based
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
~$100K-375K equity
CONTRACT GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
Known gate figures: liquid $150-200K down
01WHAT IT IS
Independent Service Provider contractor routes bought and sold on the resale market; you run FedEx Ground delivery/linehaul routes as a contractor, not a franchisee.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $0. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $250K-$1.5M+. Route purchase ~$75K-$120K per single route; most buy packages; capital needs $100K-$500K+. Vehicle replacement is a major recurring cost.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Decades-long history, strong equity value, active resale market - routes sell at 60-85% of annual revenue (~2.5-4.5x EBITDA). "Network 2.0" consolidation is pushing ISPs toward larger scale. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
P&D route margins ~10-25%; linehaul ~15-45%. On $1M revenue expect ~$140K-$180K net (14-18% typical currently); a single route grossing ~$100K nets ~$30K-$40K; average multi-route owner grosses ~$2.3M.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - contract-based. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 003▶

Bread routes (Flowers, Bimbo)

Routes & distributorships · Vol I GOOD FIT
Buy-in fee
distributorship buy-in
All-in cost
$55K-$500K
Royalty+ad
n/a - product markup
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
route price
DISTRIB HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
Known gate figures: liquid 10-30% down
01WHAT IT IS
Independent distributorship buying branded bakery products wholesale and reselling in a protected territory. Owners: Flowers Foods (Nature's Own, Dave's Killer Bread, Wonder) and Grupo Bimbo (Sara Lee, Arnold, Thomas', Entenmann's).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: distributorship buy-in. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $55K-$500K. Established branded route $50K-$350K+ (metro routes can exceed $200K). Cargo van/box truck $15K-$30K if not included. 10-20% down; company financing common.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Highly stable, recession-resistant DSD. Active resale market (BizBuySell, Route Exchange). Flowers gives up to 15% stale credit. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Commission ~18-25% of sales (Bimbo 18-22%; small cash accounts 20-30%). Net income $45K-$135K; routes sell at 12-30x weekly gross / 80-100% of annual revenue.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - product markup. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Snack/chip routes (Utz, Snyder's, Herr's)

DISTRIB HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
Known gate figures: liquid Down + credit
01WHAT IT IS
Direct-store-delivery snack distributor routes (Utz, Snyder's, Herr's) reselling product to retail; margin-based independent distributorships.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: distributorship buy-in. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $85K-$425K. Can start from scratch under $10,000 - the lowest of the route types; van costs similar to bread routes.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Stable DSD; Snyder's-Lance owned by Campbell's, Utz publicly traded. Active resale; saturation risk in mature metros. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Commission ~18-22%; high-volume Utz distributors report $10K-$13K weekly gross yielding $2K-$2.6K weekly income; net $40K-$120K. Better schedule than bread (start 6-7 AM, not 3-4 AM).
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - product markup. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 005▶

Matco Tools (tool truck)

Routes & distributorships · Vol I GOOD FIT
Buy-in fee
$8K
All-in cost
$77K-$314K
Royalty+ad
none
Net worth req
~$35K
Liquid req
$25-40K
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~90%/yr TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $35-75K; liquid $15-40K
01WHAT IT IS
Mobile automotive tool distributor route (Vontier); you sell tools from a truck to mechanics on a protected call list, with 1,600+ distributors.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $8K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $77K-$314K. Total investment $108K-$383K; starter inventory $55,500-$92,000; franchise fee $10K; royalty 8%.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
RED FLAG - 220 distributors closed/ceased in 2023 (189 in 2021); FDD discloses multiple lawsuits (fraud, misrepresentation claims). Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2025 FDD, ~1,502 distributors): average total completed business ~$526K (2024); top-third ~$766K; estimated earnings ~$49K-$59K - lower after truck lease, fuel, and financing.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.
CASE FILE 006▶

Snap-on Tools (tool truck)

Routes & distributorships · Vol I GOOD FIT

~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req

FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $40-55K; liquid ~$44K
01WHAT IT IS
Mobile tool-truck franchise selling premium tools to technicians on a protected weekly call route. Parent: Snap-on Inc. (NYSE: SNA). ~3,200+ US franchised units.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$500K. Total investment $222K-$500K (avg ~$333K); the mobile store truck is the big-ticket item, no real estate; franchise fee $8K-$16K; low flat royalty.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Very low failure rate (~0.5% went out of business in 2022); founded 1920, franchising since 1991. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 - $832,626 average Paid Sales (2024 FDD, 2,889 franchisees operating all 12 months of 2023). Net margins 10-25%; realistic owner take-home ~$48K-$130K with wide dispersion.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

01WHAT IT IS
Mobile tool franchise with geographic (not call-list) territories. Parent: Cornwell Quality Tools Co. (private); founded 1919. ~795 US units.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $8K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $109K-$250K. Total investment ~$115K-$321K; truck + inventory; franchise fee $0; veterans get up to $60K interest-free financing.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Smallest of the four tool majors, selective/slow growth; family/employee-owned; currently limiting new inquiries. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Gross revenue ~$599K-$600K; estimated earnings ~$60K-$72K.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.
CASE FILE 008▶

Vending machine route

Routes & distributorships · Vol I SIDE INCOME
Buy-in fee
route purchase price
All-in cost
$2-10K/machine
Royalty+ad
n/a - markup on product
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
ROUTE ABSENTEE-OK SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Owner-operated portfolio of snack/beverage machines placed on host-location revenue-share. ~$7.7B US industry, ~18,000 operators.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: route purchase price. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $2-10K/machine. Machines $2,000-$8,000 (smart/AI $10K-$15K+); ~$5,000-$8,000 all-in per placement; route acquisition $20K-$100K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
No franchise required; resale via business marketplaces with no standard valuation - verify seller sales claims. Softening consumer willingness to pay vending premiums is a watch item. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Per machine gross $150-$400/mo at average sites; net margin 20-35%, so ~$40-$120/machine/mo average and $300-$600 at strong sites. Roughly 21 average machines (or 13-15 premium ones) to net $100K/yr.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Semi-passive or manager-run from early on. One of the more genuinely absentee-friendly models here - though a good manager is still everything.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - markup on product. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 009▶

ATM placement / ISO

Routes & distributorships · Vol I SIDE INCOME
Buy-in fee
route purchase price
All-in cost
$3-10K/machine
Royalty+ad
n/a - markup on product
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
ROUTE ABSENTEE-OK SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Own and operate ATMs in host retail locations, keeping surcharge revenue less a 20-35% merchant split. An ISO/placement business, not a franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: route purchase price. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $3-10K/machine. Machine $2,000-$8,000 (refurb 20-30% less); vault cash $3,000-$5,000/machine; processing $20-$50/mo; connectivity $15-$25/mo.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Mature, stable; cash-heavy niches (bars, clubs, dispensaries) stay resilient; scalable but entirely placement-dependent. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Surcharge $2.50-$3.50/transaction; 150-300 transactions/mo per average machine; net $200-$600/machine/mo typical, $800-$1,000+ at high-traffic sites; a 5-machine route can clear $30K+/yr. Payback 6-18 months.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Semi-passive or manager-run from early on. One of the more genuinely absentee-friendly models here - though a good manager is still everything.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - markup on product. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 010▶

Amazon FBA (self-run)

Productized / logistics · Vol I CAUTION
Buy-in fee
none
All-in cost
$500-$10K
Royalty+ad
n/a - self-built
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
SELF-START HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Sell private-label or resale products through Amazon's Fulfillment-by-Amazon program; a self-run e-commerce business, not a franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: none. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $500-$10K. The buy-in fee alone is none, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - self-built. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - self-built. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: none / not a franchise. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.

CASE FILE 013▶

Christmas Decor

Home-services bolt-ons & franchises · Vol I TOP FIT
Buy-in fee
$9,900
All-in cost
$19.5K-$62K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $100K; liquid $50K
01WHAT IT IS
Holiday lighting. Counter-seasonal, reuses crews/trucks. Fee $9,900; ~$226K avg gross, ~15% margin, ~2.5-yr payback.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $9,900. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $19.5K-$62K. The buy-in fee alone is $9,900, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Bin There Dump That (dumpsters)

Home-services bolt-ons & franchises · Vol I GOOD FIT
Buy-in fee
$25K
All-in cost
from ~$73K
Royalty+ad
flat/truck fee
Net worth req
$100K
Liquid req
$60K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH n/a LIQUID ~
Known gate figures: liquid ~$50K
01WHAT IT IS
Residential-friendly dumpster rental franchise. ~227 locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around from ~$73K. Item 7 ~$75K-$260K. Roll-off trucks, dumpster bins (main capex), driveway-protection gear, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
227 locations; most affordable entry in the segment. Growing. Data-limited on full AUV. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
No Item 19 FPR disclosed. 1851 cites average revenue per truck ~$273,595. Recurring multi-day rental model. Dumpster net 20-30% (low labor).
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: flat/truck fee. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner/manager-run dumpster model; competing-business non-compete only.

est. $25-50K

7% + 2%
Net worth req

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH ~ LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $200K ~; liquid $75K
01WHAT IT IS
Junk-removal and moving franchise with 370+ locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $90K-$355K. Item 7 combined $203K-$355K (junk only $158K-$252K; moving only $211K-$335K). Franchise fee $55K-$75K. Box trucks/wraps, dollies/equipment, labor, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
191 units, systemwide sales $280M (2024, +5.3%); 70% of franchisees multi-unit. Growing. 88 NPS. Green flag. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2024 data) AUV ~$1.5M ($1.44-1.45M per franchise); top 25% $3.09M with $309K average EBITDA (~21% at top); 3+ year units $1.8M; 1-3 yr $734K. Junk/moving net 15-25%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 7% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-operator especially for the first location, then scales semi-absentee; no bar on unrelated ownership.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req ~$150K; liquid ~$50K
01WHAT IT IS
Wildlife/nuisance removal.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around ~$50K-$150K. The buy-in fee alone is $25K, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Two Men and a Truck

Home-services bolt-ons & franchises · Vol I CAUTION
Buy-in fee
$30
All-in cost
$100K-$277K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
$400K
Liquid req
$150K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH ~ LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $150-250K ~; liquid ~$75K
01WHAT IT IS
Largest franchised local moving company. ~350-441 locations, 3,000 trucks.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $30. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $100K-$277K. Item 7 $165K-$539K. Franchise fee $30K-$85K; 6% royalty + 1% ad. Moving-truck fleet (lease/loan), warehouse, movers, insurance, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
206 Franchise Operating Units (2024); surpassed 10M lifetime moves; record revenue 2024. Growing (esp. California). #1 in moving category 3 yrs. Green flag. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 - 2023 median revenue for a Metro Market franchise was $2,314,231 (195 locations); average EBITDA for a single franchise was $324,267 (166 single franchises). (BizBuySell cites a 2024 average unit revenue of $2,862,607, but this higher figure is not corroborated in primary sources - verify against the current FDD.) Moving net ~12-18%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Manager/team-driven, can shift to oversight; own other non-competing brands freely.

01WHAT IT IS
Outdoor mosquito/tick/pest control franchise, Neighborly. ~410-415 US units.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $115K-$192K. Item 7 $151K-$193K. Franchise fee $42.5K; 6% royalty. Spray trucks/ATVs, tanks, chemicals, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~410 units; 24 closed in 2024 (seasonal churn). Recurring seasonal contracts (green flag) but below broader pest sub-sector average. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2024 data) average revenue ~$347K-$364K per unit; gross sales per treatment ~$85. Seasonal. Pest net ~15-20% → ~$61K-$81K.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Neighborly/portfolio-style manager-run model; owning other non-competing brands is common. In-term non-compete covers competing services only.

01WHAT IT IS

Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $392K-$529K. Item 7 $392K-$529K (highest in lawn category - heavy trucks/chippers/bucket equipment). Franchise fee $49,500. Trucks, chippers, cranes, safety gear, crews, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
77 franchisees / 176 territories; growing territory count. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 ~$386K average per territory (2023); per-franchised-business figures much higher (~$883K est.) since most run 2+ territories; top 50% ~$1.2M. Tree-service net ~15-20%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. 03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $110K-$155K. The buy-in fee alone is $14,900, with ongoing royalty/ad running 3-6.5%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 3-6.5%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

$14,900

$140K-$197K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
$250K
Liquid req
$50-65K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $250K; liquid $50K
01WHAT IT IS
Residential cleaning franchise, Neighborly. 448 US units (Dec 31, 2024).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $14,900. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $140K-$197K. Item 7 $140K-$197K. 3-6.5% license + 2% MAP. Office, cleaning supplies, marked vehicles, staff, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
464 (2023) → 448 (2024) - modest 3.4% contraction. Roughly flat. Lowest franchise fee ($14,900). Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 median gross sales ~$492,547 (2024); AUV cited $759K-$793K (blended); estimated owner earnings ~$69K-$89K. Cleaning net ~16%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Neighborly/portfolio-style manager-run model; owning other non-competing brands is common. In-term non-compete covers competing services only.

Known gate figures: NW req ~$60-150K; liquid ~$50K
01WHAT IT IS
Residential cleaning franchise, ServiceMaster Brands. 802 US units (Dec 31, 2024).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $127K-$170K. Item 7 $127K-$170K. Franchise fee ~$62K. Office, supplies, vehicles, staff, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
RED FLAG - accelerating contraction: 989 → 802 US units over 3 years (−18.9%). The 2025 FDD notes 90 franchised businesses ceased operating in the covered period (one third-party analysis attributed 73 of the closures to 2024 specifically; either way, closures are worsening year over year). Survival signal is POOR here (low / shrinking / defunct) - treat as high-risk and dig hard before committing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 - Qualified Franchise average gross sales $487,441 (median $427,425); 419 "Legacy Franchises" average only ~$256K. Net ~16% → ~$68K owner earnings (~$35K-$95K range) - economically challenging for owner-operators.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Manager-run cleaning; own other non-competing brands freely.

CleanNet USA

Cleaning (specialty / residential / commercial) · Vol I AVOID
Buy-in fee
est. $25-50K
All-in cost
$5-40K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL SHRINKING TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH n/a LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Shrinking 5,274 to 1,746 units.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $5-40K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival signal is POOR here (low / shrinking / defunct) - treat as high-risk and dig hard before committing. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

H&R Block

Tax prep (counter-seasonal) · Vol I GOOD FIT
Buy-in fee
est. $25-50K
All-in cost
$32K-$158K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL HIGH ~92% TERR: RADIUS OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $100K; liquid $50K
01WHAT IT IS
Tax preparation + bookkeeping/payroll. H&R Block Inc (NYSE: HRB); founded 1955. ~2,135-2,339 franchised + ~6,582 company locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $32K-$158K. Total investment $33,824-$158,344 (new territory); franchise fee $2,500; office/build-out, tax software (franchisor), signage, seasonal staff, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Very large, mature; company-owned majority; stable, low-growth industry. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
NO Item 19 disclosed (third-party median AUV estimate ~$131K); tax prep nets ~20-30% but is seasonal; the steep ~30% tiered royalty (declining to 20%) + product royalties compresses owner margin.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Known gate figures: NW req ~$100K; liquid ~$20K
01WHAT IT IS
Mobile shaved-ice trucks (patented Flavorwave) for events/schools/fundraisers. Parent: Kona Ice, Inc. (founder-owned). ~1,820-1,933 franchised trucks - the largest US food-truck franchise; founded 2007.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $102K-$227K. Mobile - total investment $102,365-$226,841; the KEV truck itself is ~$150K-$160K. No rent/storefront. Franchise fee $15K. Requires only ~$20K-$50K liquid.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~1,820-2,500 units; per Franchise Business Review, 97% of franchisees would recommend Kona Ice and it earned a 99/100 satisfaction score (FBR award winner every year since 2014). Strong green flags. Seasonal in cold climates. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Franchisor-cited avg gross ~$150,000/truck; ~$75K EBITDA for an owner-operator; food cost only ~6%. Flat annual royalty ($3,000 yrs 1-2, rising to $5,000 yrs 7-10) - not a percentage, so operators keep more of each incremental dollar.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: flat $/yr. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-operated truck model; own other non-competing brands freely.

01WHAT IT IS
Largest US sandwich chain; ~18,773 US units (end-2025); parent Roark Capital (acquired 2023).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $150K-$328K. Total investment $190,000-$522,000. Sandwich prep line, cooler, oven, POS, signage. Franchise fee $15K. Royalty 8% (highest of the subs).
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
RED FLAG - accelerating contraction: lost ~443 net US units in 2023, ~631 in 2024 (ending 19,502), and ~729 in 2025 (steepest since 2021), ending ~18,773. Footprint down ~3,400 units since start of 2021 and ~7,600 since the ~27,000 peak in 2015. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2024 AUV a record-high but still only ~$490,000. Low-margin at that volume; high 8% royalty + ad. Realistic ~$30K-$60K/unit - weak single-unit economics. No hood/limited build helps.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Low-complexity, multi-unit friendly, manager-run capable; competing-sub non-compete only.

01WHAT IT IS

All-in cost lands around $178K-$1.16M. ~515 sq ft, avg build ~$515K; total investment $436,176-$1.162M. Slicers, prep line, grill, POS, signage. Franchise fee ~$18.5K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
20 consecutive years of same-store-sales growth; +238-254 net units in 2025; 92/100 franchisee satisfaction. Strong green flags. Minor: CA wage-hour class action, a DOL child-labor finding at a franchisee. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
AUV ~$1.36M-$1.4M (2025); ~6% of units >$2M. 16% store-level margin on ~$1.4M AUV; average cash-on-cash returns cited over 40%. Realistic ~$180K-$250K. Royalty 6.5% + 5% ad.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
No absentee ownership - owner or trained manager must run it - but you can still own unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
World's largest burger QSR; ~13,457 US units (mostly franchised); parent McDonald's Corp (public).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $1.47M-$2.73M. Full kitchen + drive-thru $1M-$2.2M (excl. land): grills/fryers, dual drive-thru, POS/kiosks, signage, inventory, plus McDonald's-owned real estate. New franchises rare; most entry via resale.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~13,457 US units, essentially flat (+13 in reference year); extremely stable; US system sales $53.1B (highest of any US chain). No systemic red flags. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
US AUV ~$4.0M (2023). QSR burger nets ~6-10% after the ~4% service fee plus McDonald's-controlled rent (often 8-12% of sales, unusually high). Realistic franchisee cash flow ~$150K-$300K per store; most owners run multiple. Requires ~$500K liquid; total investment ~$1.5M-$2.5M+.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Demanding owner-operator model (no passive investors), but nothing bars you from owning other unrelated franchises.

$10K fee only
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS EXCLUSIVE – NO OTHERS NET WORTH ~ LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Chicken QSR where the company funds build-out and retains ownership; you pay only a $10K non-borrowed fee and operate the restaurant.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $10K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $427K-$2.3M. N/A to franchisee - CFA owns real estate and equipment; operator investment is just the ~$10K fee.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~2,964-3,100 US units, growing ~140-180/yr; US system sales ~$22B (2024). Strong green flags throughout; the only investor "negative" is that you cannot build a portfolio. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Per the 2024 FDD (filed April 1, 2025), of ~2,179 domestic non-mall franchised restaurants, median annual sales volume was $9.227M and average $9.317M (highest store $19.319M); total system AUV ~$7.4M (Technomic). Unique model: CFA funds the build; the operator pays only a ~$10K franchise fee but keeps a much smaller share (~$200K-$300K take-home) because CFA takes ~15% of sales plus ~50% of net profit. Not a portfolio-scaling model - one unit per operator, no absentee ownership.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
The one brand that bars a portfolio: requires a full-time, hands-on operator with no other active business ventures.
CASE FILE 057▶

Anytime Fitness (gym)

Storefront-heavy (parked) · Vol I PARKED
Buy-in fee
est. $25-50K
All-in cost
$398K-$973K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
$500K
Liquid req
$100K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
Known gate figures: NW req $380K; liquid $225K
01WHAT IT IS
24/7 keyless convenience gym; ~2,282 US units; parent Purpose Brands.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $398K-$973K. Total $539K-$905K. Equipment pack, ~4,000-5,500 sq ft build-out, signage, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~2,282 US units, declining three straight years (2,310→2,301→2,282); low termination rates - a mature system in slow contraction, not collapse. Part of Purpose Brands (~7,000 locations with Orangetheory). Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2025 FDD median gross revenue $399K (n=1,683); franchimp cites median AUV $451,953. EBITDA margins 20-35% (flat ~$842/mo royalty, low staffing). Realistic owner take-home ~$80K-$180K at a mature single club.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Lean 24/7 model, GM-run; own other non-competing brands freely.
CASE FILE 058▶

Planet Fitness (gym)

Storefront-heavy (parked) · Vol I PARKED
Buy-in fee
est. $25-50K
All-in cost
$1.6M-$5.2M
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
$3M+
Liquid req
$1.5M+
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
Known gate figures: NW req $3M; liquid $1.5M
01WHAT IT IS
High-value-low-price big-box gym; 2,896 clubs system-wide; public company.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $1.6M-$5.2M. Total $1.5M-$5.16M - highest in the category. Large-format build-out (~20,000 sq ft), extensive cardio, HVAC. 18-36 month ramp.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
2,896 total system-wide clubs as of Dec 31, 2025 (global, not US-only), with total membership of ~20.8 million (up 1.1 million for the year). Aggressive growth and demonstrated pricing power. Red flags: ~30 active Item 3 litigation cases; no exclusive territory. Public (NYSE: PLNT). Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2024 EFT-revenue tiers - top third $2.6M, middle $1.8M, bottom $1.2M. EBITDA margins 30-40%; owner cash flow $350K-$700K+ at a strong club.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
GM-run per club; multi-unit/multi-brand common. Competing-gym non-compete only.

01WHAT IT IS
Small-format 24/7 gym; ~965 US units; parent Lift Brands (for sale).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $431K-$1.12M. Total $431K-$1.1M. Franchise fee $39,500. Equipment, ~4,000-6,000 sq ft build-out, signage.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~965 US units, declining (1,036→965); ~60-70 net closures over 12-18 months. Red flag: parent Lift Brands is actively for sale, creating franchisor-stability risk for new 10-year agreements. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2025 FDD median AUV ~$239K (n=965); top quartile ~$380K, bottom ~$140K. Unmanned 24/7 model keeps labor <15% but caps the ceiling; thin owner take-home ~$25K-$60K single unit.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Lean 24/7 gym, GM-run; competing-gym non-compete only.

01WHAT IT IS
World's leading quick-lube. Parent: Shell (SOPUS). ~1,721-2,230 US units.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $207K-$443K. Total investment ~$207K-$500K for existing sites, more for ground-up; real estate/build-out, bays/pits, lube and light-repair equipment, POS.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Very large, stable; strong brand. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2023 FDD): system mean net adjusted sales $1,008,618; median $923,291; top quartile $1.54M; nets ~15-20%. Lowest fixed royalty in category (4%) + 4% minimum advertising.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Men's and boys' haircut franchise with a sports theme; 1,800+ locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $188K-$475K. Total $289K-$475K. Franchise fee $25,000. Salon build-out, sports-theme decor, TVs, stations.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~1,799 US units, declining (1,900+→1,872→1,799; ~127 net closures over two years). Red flag: ~26 active litigation cases. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2024 Item 19 avg AUV ~$419K, median ~$409K; owner earnings ~$82K/unit (most own 3-10). Royalty 6% + 5% ad = 11% burden.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Manager-run haircare; owners often keep other work. Competing-haircare non-compete only.
CASE FILE 062▶

MLMs (Mary Kay, Avon, Herbalife, Amway...)

MLM / direct sales · Vol I AVOID
Buy-in fee
starter kit
All-in cost
$50-$500 kit
Royalty+ad
n/a - commission
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
MLM HANDS-ON SURVIVAL VERY LOW TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Cheapest entry, worst odds. FTC: most participants make under $1,000/yr.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: starter kit. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50-$500 kit. The buy-in fee alone is starter kit, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - commission. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival signal is POOR here (low / shrinking / defunct) - treat as high-risk and dig hard before committing. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - commission. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Confirm what marketing the company actually funds versus what falls on you.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

est. $25-50K

$150-300K
Royalty+ad
4%
Net worth req
$200K
Liquid req
$75K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
Known gate figures: NW req $250K; liquid $150K
01WHAT IT IS
Plumbing and HVAC service franchise combining drain, water-heater and heating/cooling work under one brand.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $150-300K. Item 7 ~$127,780-$284,270; franchise fee $49,500; royalty 6%. Vans, HVAC + plumbing equipment, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
40+ units; emerging/growing. Data-limited - verify Item 19 directly. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
No widely published Item 19 AUV located. Category fallback: plumbing/HVAC AUV $1.3M-$1.75M, net 10-15%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 4%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

All-in cost lands around $146-285K. Item 7 $104,930-$158,850; franchise fee $64,950. Showroom optional (can be home-based), sample displays, van, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
47 franchised + 5 company units (2023); emerging/growing. Data-limited AUV. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 shows a very low reported figure ($18,057) reflecting early-stage ramp; sister-brand economics suggest ~$360K-$500K AUV at maturity, ~15% net (~$54K EBITDA at $360K).

Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.

09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

All-in cost lands around $114-272K. Item 7 $113,808-$271,708. Franchise fee $45,000 + population adder. Vans, HVAC equipment, licensing, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~197 units, 30+ years (since 1993). Stable/slow growth. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2025 FDD, 158 reporting businesses) average gross sales ~$1,499,969. HVAC net ~10-15% → ~$150K-$225K.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 5-7%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Neighborly/portfolio-style manager-run model; owning other non-competing brands is common. In-term non-compete covers competing services only.

01WHAT IT IS

Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $122-264K. Item 7 ~$122K-$264K. Franchise fee ~$42.5K. Vans/wraps, tools, licensing, 8% total fee burden (6% royalty + 2% ad).
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
229 US units end of 2024; stable-to-modest growth. #104 Entrepreneur Franchise 500 (2025). Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 median AUV ~$1.3M (2024 data, 188 reporting); average ~$1.75M. ~13.5% net → ~$152K-$191K owner earnings.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.

Neighborly/portfolio-style manager-run model; owning other non-competing brands is common. In-term non-compete covers competing services only.

Net worth req

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID YES
