01WHAT IT IS
IT services + on-site residential and SMB tech support/repair (retail + mobile). Emerging system.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $45,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $132K-$243K. Total investment ~$150K-$300K; retail/service build-out, service vehicle, diagnostic tools, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Emerging/growing system - thinner data. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 availability limited; blends retail repair (~10-15% net) with recurring managed-services revenue.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 10%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

CASE FILE 503▶

Money Mailer

Advertising / Direct Mail / Marketing · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
$45,000
All-in cost
$58K-$97K
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Shared/direct-mail advertising ("Blue Envelope"). ~21 franchised + ~110 company units after 2020-2023 ownership changes.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $45,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $58K-$97K. Total investment ~$65K-$79K; franchise fee $59,900; home/office-based, mailing-float working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
RED FLAG - heavy shift toward company-owned; historically ~168 franchised units, now ~21. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 AUV ~$626,147, but a steep 20% royalty + $1,000/yr and postage/print costs compress margins hard.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

05OWNER PROFIT

06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.
CASE FILE 512▶

Smooth Move

Estate Sales / Downsizing / Transition · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
varies
All-in cost
low
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Senior move management; asset-light, referral-driven.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around low. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.
CASE FILE 513▶

Goosehead Insurance

varies
Net worth req
not published
Liquid req
$25-50K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Independent personal-lines insurance agency (home/auto/flood intermediary). Public (NASDAQ: GSHD). ~1,022-1,115 franchised locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25K-$60K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $40K-$120K. Total investment $40K-$118K; office, licensing, E&O insurance, agency software, producer payroll, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
YELLOW FLAG on unit count - contracted from ~1,115 to ~1,022 locations in 2025, though producer count grew; #1 insurance franchise on the 2024 Franchise 500. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2025 FDD): franchised businesses avg ~$113K revenue; commission-based and high-margin, especially renewals - franchisee keeps 80% of new-business and 50% of renewal commissions; ~86% retention means the renewal book compounds.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner builds a book and hires staff; competing-insurance non-compete only.

01WHAT IT IS
Independent insurance agency (property/auto/life/business), multi-carrier. Jacksonville FL; founded 2003. ~333-338 franchised locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$100K. Total investment $44K-$160K; franchise fee $50,000; office, licensing, E&O insurance, agency software, producer payroll.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Steady ~16% growth; $1.7B+ written premium after the GlobalGreen acquisition; did not rank on the 2026 Franchise 500 (minor flag). Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2026 FDD): established 5+ year agencies avg ~$681K gross commissions; franchisee retains 80% new / 50-55% renewal commissions; healthy net on the renewal book.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Agency model, staff-run capable; competing-insurance non-compete only.

AGENCY GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: OPEN MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH ~ LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Captive insurance agency (Allstate exclusive agent) - an agency appointment, not a classic franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: none (buy book). This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around buy-in varies. ~$100K+ liquid capital typically required to start or acquire a book; office, licensing, E&O, staff; no classic franchise fee.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Very large captive network. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Commission-based on Allstate products; established agencies commonly generate solid six-figure owner income on a mature book, with economic interest in the book; no FDD/Item 19.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: commission. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Carrier brand plus your local grind. Referral partnerships (mortgage, real-estate, community networking) drive the book; the company supplies product access and credibility, not clients.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Tech-forward residential real estate brokerage franchise. Pleasanton CA. ~414+ offices.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $8K-$28K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $27K-$213K. Total investment $14,750-$213,100 (virtual to full office); franchise fee $3,750-$8,000; office/build-out optional, tech, signage, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Grew rapidly from 2014 launch to 414+ offices. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
No robust Item 19 (FDD discloses lawsuits/bankruptcy); brokerage revenue comes from agent commission splits/fees; broker-owner net depends on agent count. Low flat royalty ($180-$190/mo + per-transaction ad fee).
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.

Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.

Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: OPEN MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH ~ LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Real estate investing franchise ("We Buy Ugly Houses") - buy/rehab/sell/rent. Dallas; franchising since 1996.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $52K-$80K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $60K-$450K. Franchise investment moderate, but the real capital need is acquisition/rehab capital - hundreds of thousands per deal; marketing, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Long-established; historically among fastest-growing franchises. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 in FDD; income is deal-margin on flips/rentals - highly capital- and market-dependent, lumpy; Full vs. Associate models differ.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: $/deal. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Marketed to portfolio investors; competing-REI non-compete only.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: OPEN MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Short-term/vacation rental property management franchise; home-based.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $45,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$100K. Low - ~$55K-$110K; STR software, marketing, working capital, cleaning/vendor network.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Established STR-management system. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
No robust public Item 19; STR management nets ~10-20% of managed booking revenue (management fees typically 10-30% of rental income); scales with property count.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

All-in cost lands around $60K-$130K. Low-moderate - home/office-based; STR software, marketing, working capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Growing STR-management brand. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
No robust public Item 19; STR management nets ~10-20% of managed revenue; recurring, scales with door count.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. 09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.

Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Transmission specialty + total car care. Parent: American Driveline. ~527-536 North American units.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $230K-$400K. Total investment $226,300-$330,500; franchise fee $39,500; lifts, bays, transmission diagnostic/rebuild equipment (five-to-six-figure specialty tooling), build-out.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Largest transmission franchise; mature. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Aggregator average revenue ~$713,092/unit; estimated ~19% net, roughly $135K profit. Royalty 7.5%. Transmission work = high skill barrier, low visit frequency.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 7.5%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Auto/home/business glass repair and replacement. Parent: Neighborly. ~131-185 units; founded 1962.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $30,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $130K-$300K. Total investment ~$152,900-$326,850; franchise fee $59,900; service vehicles, glass-cutting/installation tools, inventory, build-out for the shop model.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Established Neighborly brand; stable. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19: avg gross sales ~$969K (home & business), ~$677K-$781K (auto); glass nets ~10-15%. Royalty 5% + 2%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES

09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.

Neighborly/portfolio-style manager-run model; owning other non-competing brands is common. In-term non-compete covers competing services only.

varies

n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
DEALER GM-ABLE SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NEGOTIATED MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Spray-on protective coatings/bedliners. NOT A FRANCHISE - dealer/licensee model with no franchise or marketing fees.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $30K-$150K. Equipment ~$10K-$30K; total startup well under six figures; spray rig, compressor, PPE, initial product.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Large dealer network (not franchised). Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside.
05OWNER PROFIT
No FDD/Item 19; dealer margins on coatings/application; low overhead.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

01WHAT IT IS
Mobile bike repair (Sprinter-van workshops). Vancouver; founded 2012. ~130 across North America.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $80K-$150K. Total investment $203,000-$252,000; franchise fee $25K-$35K; fully-outfitted service van (the major cost), bike tools/equipment, parts inventory.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Established mobile niche. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
No published Item 19; mobile bike-service net is moderate and seasonal. Royalty 8% + 2%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 8%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Stay-in-your-car drive-thru quick-lube franchise (Driven Brands) with 1,000+ locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $35,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$1.5M. 2024 FDD: total investment for a new ground-up center $749,292-$2,033,733. Big-ticket: land/real estate, tunnel/pit build-out, lube equipment, signage; franchise fee $30,000.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Rapid growth under Driven Brands; category disruptor. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
No detailed public unit economics from the franchisor; industry reports ~$400K-$1M+/location; quick-lube nets ~10-15%+ before royalties. Royalty 6%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Manager-run quick-lube; competing-lube non-compete only.

01WHAT IT IS
Quick-lube preventive-maintenance centers. Valvoline Inc. ~1,100-1,810 US centers (No. 2 quick-lube chain by store count).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $50,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$2M. Total investment ~$192K-$3.48M (conversion vs. ground-up with real estate); real estate, bays/pits, lube equipment, signage; franchise fee $30,000.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Large, growing, strong brand. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19-cited average annual revenue per unit $1,609,279 (median $1,528,074) in 2024; quick-lube nets ~10-20% of net sales, with ~16.8% operating profit cited. Royalty 5-6%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Manager-run quick-lube multi-unit; competing-lube non-compete only.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Drive-thru quick-lube. Winston-Salem; founded 2016. ~150-226+ units, growing fast.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $400K-$1.2M. Total investment $217,900-$354,900 (up to ~$2.1M with ground-lease/build); franchise fee $54,900; bays, lube equipment, build-out, signage.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Rapid growth - system sales up 65.5% to ~$116M, 226 locations - but a young system with a short track record. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 (2024 FDD): average revenue ~$840K, median ~$814K (small sample of 10); quick-lube nets ~15-20%. Royalty 6% + 2%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Membership boat club (members reserve/use a shared fleet). Parent: Brunswick Corporation (NYSE: BC). ~266 franchised + 129 company units; 50,000+ memberships; founded 1989.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $50,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $170K-$490K. Total investment ~$223K-$501K; franchise fee $50K; royalty 6% + up to 1% ad; the boat fleet, dock/slip leases, and insurance are the big-ticket items.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Largest/oldest boat club; NOTE - Brunswick has been buying back franchise territories. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
No Item 19 disclosed (the franchisor omits a financial performance representation). Recurring membership model - entry fees + monthly dues; category-typical established-club net income $75K-$250K depending on member count/fleet.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Membership model, manager-run capable; own other non-competing brands freely.

CASE FILE 543▶

Boat detailing / dealer

Marine / Recreation / Boat · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
varies

low
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
SELF-START HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Marine detailing; owner-operator, seasonal, low overhead.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around low. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: OPEN OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
B2B third-party logistics/freight and small-parcel reseller (non-asset). Parent: Worldwide Express; office-based sales.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$120K. Low - office-based; franchise fee, office, sales staff, working capital; no shipping assets.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Large 3PL network, stable. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Revenue is commission on shipping volume; recurring and high-retention; income scales with the account book.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

CONTRACT HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Contracted same-day/last-mile courier work for a managed logistics platform (Dropoff). Not a franchise or route sale.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around varies. Vehicle, insurance, smartphone; minimal capital.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Venture-backed B2B same-day delivery firm operating in multiple US metros. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Gig-contracting economics - per-delivery pay; not an equity-building route.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Growing with lab volumes; fragmented, contract-dependent. Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%).
05OWNER PROFIT
Solo couriers commonly net $40K-$90K; fleet owners more. Recurring lab/pharmacy contracts.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$120K. The buy-in fee alone is $30,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF

Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.

Semi-absentee / manager-run, and the non-compete covers competing businesses only - it slots cleanly into a multi-brand portfolio.

04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Large installed base at major retailers. Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside.
05OWNER PROFIT
Margin on water exchange/refill is thin per unit but recurring; the "dealer" relationship is typically retail placement/distribution, not a franchise.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

DEALER HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NEGOTIATED MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH ~ LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Retail propane distribution and tank service to homes/businesses.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $100K-$400K. Bobtail delivery truck $80K-$150K+; bulk storage; customer tanks; DOT/hazmat licensing - capital intensive.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Stable, consolidating (AmeriGas, Suburban Propane dominate; independents sell at premiums). The recurring customer base is the asset. Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside.
05OWNER PROFIT
Per-gallon margin often $0.80-$1.50+/gal delivered plus tank rental; established dealers net $75K-$250K+. Seasonal.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

01WHAT IT IS
Bulk mulch delivery and pneumatic blow-in installation for landscaping.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: n/a. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $40K-$150K. The blower truck is the big-ticket item ($60K-$200K new; used less); mulch inventory.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Fragmented local businesses; spring/summer peak. Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside.
05OWNER PROFIT
High-margin seasonal work; operators commonly net $50K-$150K in season.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE

04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Fast-growing but early-stage; density is everything; municipal contracts add stability. Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside.

06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF

07ROYALTY + AD FEES

Snow removal (commercial contract)

Snow / Seasonal Exterior · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
n/a
All-in cost
$30K-$200K
Royalty+ad
n/a
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
SELF-START HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Commercial plowing/salting; counter-seasonal, equipment reuse. Recurring contracts.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: n/a. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $30K-$200K. The buy-in fee alone is n/a and there is no standard percentage royalty. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 557▶

Holiday-lighting brands (various)

Snow / Seasonal Exterior · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
varies
All-in cost
$30K-$100K
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Holiday-lighting install/removal; counter-seasonal to lawn.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $30K-$100K. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.
CASE FILE 558▶

Little Caesars

Food - QSR / Pizza / Sub / Mexican · Vol IV
Buy-in fee
$20,000
All-in cost
$384K-$1.4M
Royalty+ad
6% + ad
Net worth req
$400K
Liquid req
$200K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Value pizza ($5.99 Hot-N-Ready); ~4,285 units; parent Ilitch Holdings (private).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $20,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $384K-$1.4M. Total investment $447K-$1.8M. Ovens, cooler, prep, POS, signage. Franchise fee $20K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
4,173 (2022) → 4,285 (2024) units, modest growth. RED FLAGS: ~30 active litigation cases (FDD Item 3), low ~50/100 franchisee satisfaction, major franchisee (Red Door Pizza) filed Chapter 11. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
No Item 19 disclosed. Industry estimate AUV $700K-$900K (one FDD-derived cite $980,402). Value pizza is thin-margin (~4-10%); high fee burden ~13% (6% royalty + 7% ad). Realistic ~$80K-$200K but long payback (~9-11 years).
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6% + ad. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Offers a semi-absentee model with an approved on-site operator; competing-pizza non-compete only.

01WHAT IT IS
Hot subs, first-responder brand; ~1,276 US units; parent Restaurant Brands International (RBI).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $20,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$1.1M. Total investment $200,000-$907,000 (higher w/ drive-thru). Steamers/grill, prep, POS, signage. Franchise fee $24.5K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
+47 net US units in 2025 (~1,276) - strongest net expansion in over a decade. Green flag. Development incentives offset build costs. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
AUV $960,000 (2025); franchisee profitability >$100K/unit (up from $90K in 2024). Sub shops net ~8-12%. Royalty 6%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6% + 3%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-operator sub QSR; competing-sub non-compete only.

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $80K-$700K. Total investment $204,000-$985,000. Flat-top grills, fryer, hood, POS, signage. Franchise fee ~$24.5K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
700+ locations, expanding into strip-centers/freestanding/fuel/Walmart. Green flag on multi-unit adoption. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Strip-center AUV $958,680 (franchisor). Cheesesteak QSR nets ~8-12%; over 50% of franchisees are multi-unit. Royalty 6%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
2nd-largest US Mexican QSR (tacos + burgers/fries, "QSR+"); ~594 units (461 franchised / 133 company); parent Jack in the Box Inc. (acquired 2022).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $35,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $1M+. Full kitchen + drive-thru; total investment $1,497,200-$3,321,000. Grills, fryers, hood, dual drive-thru, POS, signage. Requires $500K liquid / $1M net worth.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~594 units; 9 consecutive years of franchise same-store-sales growth. Green flag. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
AUV ~$1.6M (Item 19; one cite $1.541M). Mexican QSR nets ~6-10%; realistic ~$120K-$200K/unit. Royalty 5%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 5% + ad. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.
CASE FILE 565▶

7-Eleven

Food - QSR / Pizza / Sub / Mexican · Vol IV
Buy-in fee
varies
All-in cost
$70K-$1.2M
Royalty+ad
GP split
Net worth req
$150K
Liquid req
$50-150K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS EXCLUSIVE – NO OTHERS NET WORTH ~ LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
World's largest convenience store chain; ~7,218 US franchised units (~13,000+ US total w/ Speedway/Stripes); parent Seven & i Holdings (Japan).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $70K-$1.2M. Total investment $142,150-$1.6M+; franchise fee $0-$690,000 (varies by store gross-profit level). Store fixtures, coolers, fuel (some), POS, signage, inventory.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
RED FLAG - ~595 net US franchise closures cited (-8.1%); contracting franchised base. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
No Item 19 disclosed. C-stores run on a gross-profit split, not a simple royalty - 7-Eleven takes ~50%+ of store gross profit (aggregators list an ~18% figure). Owner take-home varies widely (~$50K-$150K) on high revenue but thin net; very hands-on.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: GP split. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
7-Eleven's applicant criteria let it reject you for other business interests it deems a conflict - confirm before applying.

02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around varies. Standard franchise $27,562-$286,820; newly-constructed model $935,000-$8,297,000. Store fixtures, coolers, fuel, POS, signage, inventory. Franchise fee $35K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~6,158+ US units; Item 20 shows a 0.0% closure rate over the reported outlet-years - stable. Green flag on stability. (Most Circle K stores are company-operated; franchising is a smaller program.) Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
AUV ~$1,309,000 (FDD-derived) on a thin c-store net (1-3% of a large revenue base). Royalty 3.5% (low, standard franchise model).
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.

01WHAT IT IS
Hand-rolled soft pretzels + lemonade (mall/travel-heavy); ~1,193 US units (1,182 franchised / 11 company), 1,700+ global; parent GoTo Foods (formerly Focus Brands).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $30,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$500K. Total investment $156,175-$638,300. Pretzel ovens, mixers, rolling stations, POS, signage. Franchise fee $35.5K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~1,193 units, mature/stable. Neutral-positive. Location dependency (mall-traffic risk). Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Avg gross revenue $877,854; median $832,359 (2025 FDD); airport units ~$1.8M, mall ~$763K. Snack retail nets ~10-15%. Royalty 7% (may rise to 8%) + ~2.5-4%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6% + 1%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Kiosk model, manager-run capable; competing-pretzel non-compete only.

Crumbl

Food - Coffee / Dessert / Ice Cream · Vol IV CAUTION
Buy-in fee
$50,000
All-in cost
$390K-$690K
Royalty+ad
8% + 2%
Net worth req
$500K
Liquid req
$200K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED - saturation TERR: RADIUS OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Rotating weekly gourmet cookie/dessert menu; ~1,000+ US units; parent TSG Consumer Partners-backed (Lindon, UT).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $50,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $390K-$690K. Total investment $816,066-$1,442,533 (real estate/improvements $350K-$700K). Ovens, mixers, dough equipment, coolers, POS, signage. Heavy build for a dessert concept.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
RED FLAGS - first-ever closures began 2023 (7), 12 in 2024, more in 2025; openings decelerated from 300+ to ~54-81/yr; TCPA class action + $24M copyright suit; ~1.7/5 franchisee satisfaction cited. High variance, novelty-fade risk. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Per Crumbl's 2026 FDD (via QSR Magazine), 2025 average gross sales $1.139M / median $1.093M (top store $3.42M, lowest $365,129), down ~16% from 2024's $1.35M; trajectory $1.84M (2022) → $1.16M (2023) → $1.35M (2024) → $1.139M (2025). 2024 median net profit ~$223K but declined 32% YoY; only ~43% of stores earn above average. Notably, Crumbl omitted profitability from the 2025 FDD for the first time. Royalty + ad ~10%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 8% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not for absentee owners - you run the first ~60 days - but no bar on owning unrelated franchises.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Tropical shaved ice ("Sno"), smoothies, açaí; ~115 units (112 franchised / 3 company); parent Bahama Buck's Franchise Corp (Buchanan family).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $250K-$700K. Total investment $528,050-$1,223,050. Shaved-ice machines, blenders, freezers, POS, signage. Franchise fee $34.5K.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~115 units; 4 consecutive years of AUV growth. Positive. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
AUV ~$576,000 (published). Shaved-ice/beverage nets ~10-15% (seasonal in cold climates). Royalty 6% + 2%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

~140 units; rebranded 2023. Neutral. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT

Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Fitness + nutrition hybrid; verify FDD.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around varies. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Membership, cash-pay chiropractic-care franchise with ~900 US clinics.

Buy-in fee: $39,900. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE

04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~967 US units (largest chiropractic network). New CEO Sanjiv Razdan (Oct 2024) refranchising corporate clinics. Public (NASDAQ: JYNT). Red flags: trademark litigation + patient suits. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2024 Item 19 avg AUV $569,571, top quartile $913K; ~17.5% net; owner earnings ~$79K-$106K. Cash-based membership, no insurance. Royalty 7% + 2-3%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Full-service, insurance-accepting chiropractic + massage + supplements; ~117-125 units; PE-backed.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $49,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $400K-$700K. Total $340K-$829K. Franchise fee ~$50K. Full-clinic build-out; requires DC (or managed model in some states).
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~117-125 US units, growing. PE growth investment from Red Iron Group (June 2024). Founded 2004. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 avg gross ~$813K-$905K (multiple streams: chiro, massage, supplements, insurance); owner earnings ~$98K-$126K. Royalty 6.5% + 10% billing fee on insurance collections.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Retail optical/eyecare centers; ~442 units; parent EssilorLuxottica.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $30,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $393K-$1M. Total $679,760-$1,268,624 (new licensed operator). Franchise fee $30K new. High retail + optical-lab build-out; requires OD on staff.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~442 US franchised units, modest growth. Owned by EssilorLuxottica. Established brand (since 1961). Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2024 Item 19 avg net revenue ~$1.24M; discloses EBITDA; gross margin ~71%; owner income ~$182K-$243K. Royalty 7% + national/local ad.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 7%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
May need a licensed optometrist on-site; ownership can stay light. Competing-optical non-compete only.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH ~ LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Hearing aids; retail + audiology support.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $30,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $130K-$400K. The buy-in fee alone is $30,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS

Buy-in fee: $35,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $100K-$200K. Total $60K-$298K (low, asset-light). Franchise fee $40K-$54.5K. Small retail draw-station build-out.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~221-244 stand-alone franchised units (600+ locations claimed), growing. Merged with ARCpoint (2024) → CRESSO Brands. LabCorp partner. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 avg gross ~$339K-$549K (wide variability); owner earnings thin (~$39K-$52K single unit). Royalty 7-8% + 2%.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 7%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Retail lab, manager-run capable; own other non-competing brands freely.

04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~575-595 US locations (largest PT franchise), strong growth. HQ Sarasota, FL. Balance/vestibular niche. Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
Item 19 franchise avg gross ~$343K-$450K; company centers >$1M. Royalty greater of 6% or tiered minimum + up to 2%.

Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.

01WHAT IT IS
Physician-supervised medical weight-loss clinics; ~104 units; PE-owned (Audax).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$400K. Total $251K-$494K. Franchise fee $60,000. Clinic build-out; physician-supervised.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
~104 units (87 franchised, 17 company), contracting/flat. PE-owned (Audax, Nov 2022). Red flags: heavy fees pressure low-volume units; non-exclusive territory; franchisor sells DTC (channel overlap). Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully.
05OWNER PROFIT
2025 Item 19 avg gross $989,143, median $883,593 (n=57); gross profit ~80%; ~15% EBITDA (~$132K). Royalty greater of 10% or $2,250/mo + $5K/mo local ad (heavy fee load).
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training.
